text: A Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) is an official document required by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) for many international study permit applications. The PAL confirms that a student has been accounted for under a provincial allocation within Canada’s national cap on international students.

Most new post-secondary international students applying for undergraduate studies at Queen’s University must provide a Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) when submitting their study permit application to IRCC, unless they are explicitly exempt.

Applicants who require a Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) must include it with their study permit application to Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC). Applications submitted without a required PAL will be returned by IRCC.

After accepting an offer of admission to Queen’s University and paying the required $2500 CAD deposit, eligible international students will receive access to their Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) through the SOLUS Student Centre.

The Provincial Attestation Letter (PAL) and the Letter of Acceptance (LOA) are two separate documents. Both documents are required to apply for a Canadian study permit through Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC).

Accepting an offer of admission to Queen’s University is separate from the study permit process. Students must review their offer carefully, accept it by the deadline, and meet all conditions before proceeding with their study permit application.

Queen’s University has received its provincial allocation and is actively issuing Provincial Attestation Letters (PALs) to eligible international students who have accepted their offers and paid the required deposit.
International students transferring from post-secondary studies to secondary school in Canada are required to apply for a new study permit under current immigration regulations.

International students are encouraged to seek guidance from Queen’s International Student Advisor, Carolina Palmer, who is regulated by the College of Canadian Immigration Consultants and trained in Canadian immigration systems.

For detailed information about study permits, visas, and residency requirements, international students should consult the Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) website and the Queen’s University International Centre (QUIC) website.

QBridge-English Language Bridging Pathway at Queen's University offers academically competitive students who do not meet English language proficiency requirements a pathway to undergraduate admission. The program ensures students are linguistically, academically, and culturally prepared for Queen's undergraduate studies.
QBridge Accelerated: An intensive eight-week summer course for students who meet a minimum standardized English language test score, designed to quickly prepare students for undergraduate studies.

QBridge English for Academic Purposes (EAP): Designed for students who do not meet QBridge Accelerated requirements but are academically competitive for admission. Focuses on improving English language proficiency to meet undergraduate entry standards.
Further Information: For program details, including language test score minimums and registration, consult Queen's School of English.

BorderPass is an immigration support service offered at no cost as part of Queen’s Commitment Package. It helps international students navigate the Canadian immigration process throughout their stay, not just in the first year.

BorderPass provides support to ensure students have all necessary guidance, access to Canadian immigration lawyers, assistance in building and reviewing visa applications, and notifications of key dates for visa extensions, work permits, and residency visas.

Working with BorderPass increases the potential for a successful study permit application by providing a secure online platform for preparation, review, and filing, expert legal guidance, continuous monitoring of visa status, and additional perks like Express Entry tracking.

BorderPass offers study permit application preparation, review, and filing services through Canadian immigration lawyers, as well as one-year access to the BorderPass platform, providing visibility into compliance and the full immigration journey.

Students receive a free annual BorderPass subscription for their first year while enrolled full-time at Queen’s. The subscription covers premium services and access to immigration lawyers, with students responsible only for government processing and biometrics fees if applicable.

Students may opt out of BorderPass at any time, but will lose access to the Document Vault, Application Hub, key date notifications, Canadian immigration lawyer support, Express Entry tracking, and other essential newcomer services.

BorderPass provides a step-by-step guide to help students track their immigration journey, with email notifications, visa monitoring and management, and a Document Vault to view all immigration documentation.

BorderPass enhances the completeness, accuracy, and professionalism of study permit applications by having them reviewed and submitted by Canadian immigration lawyers, though approval is ultimately at the discretion of IRCC.

International students can access additional immigration resources and information through the BorderPass Knowledge Base to better understand their immigration journey and specific permit application processes.

Queen's University Indigenous Student Admission Pathway offers Indigenous candidates an additional pathway for first-year full-time undergraduate admission. Admission depends on program prerequisites, supplementary information, and annual enrolment plan allocations.

Application Process: Apply through the Ontario Universities' Application Centre (OUAC), self-identify as Indigenous, provide verification of Indigenous status, meet program admission requirements, complete any supplementary applications (Commerce, Health Sciences, Nursing), and apply for residence by the stated deadline.

Identity Verification: Applicants must provide documentation or verification from a legally recognized Indigenous community. Accepted documents include Certificate of Indian Status, Métis Nation Citizenship card, Nunavut Trust Certificate, Inuit Enrollment card, First Nation citizenship identification, or US Indigenous tribe verification.

Support: Students in this pathway receive support throughout the admission process, priority admission consideration, access to need- and merit-based financial awards, residence guarantee, early move-in, and early connection to the Four Directions Indigenous Student Centre and cultural supports.

Financial Aid: Merit- and need-based financial awards are available to students who self-identify as First Nations, Inuit, or Métis and complete the two-step Indigenous verification process via OUAC and Queen's University Policy.

Outreach Programs: Queen's participates in the Aboriginal Post-Secondary Information Program (APSIP) each fall to engage communities. Verified Indigenous students from the US are eligible for waivers under the Jay Treaty tuition differential policy.
Specialized Programs: Indigenous Futures in Engineering supports Indigenous students in the Faculty of Engineering and Applied Science with specialized student services to prepare future engineers.
Recruitment Contact: Sharlene Anderson, Indigenous Recruitment Coordinator, supports Indigenous students through the admission process. Contact: sharlene.anderson@queensu.ca
